title: 20 Tiqimt — The First Entry into Egypt & The Assembly
narrative:
Tiqimt

The First Entry of Christ into Egypt (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Gerasimus of the Jordan, Desert Father

St. Artemius the Great Martyr

The twentieth day of Tiqimt carries the monthly commemoration of the First Entry of Christ into Egypt — the Flight into Egypt, that nocturnal journey of the Holy Family into the land of bondage that became, in the providence of God, a land of refuge. In the month of interiority, this feast carries a particular resonance: the most important persons in the history of salvation traveled in complete obscurity, protected not by earthly power but by a dream, guided not by a visible pillar of fire but by an interior prompting received in the vulnerability of sleep. Joseph the foster-father is Tiqimt's preeminent interior saint in this sense — the man who received divine guidance entirely in the interior register, in dreams, without a single prophetic oracle or angelic visitation in the waking state, and who obeyed what the dreams said with a promptness and completeness that stands as one of the most complete examples of docility to the divine will in the entire scriptural narrative.

St. Gerasimus of the Jordan — the fifth-century Palestinian desert father who is remembered for his relationship with the lion from whose paw he extracted a thorn — brings the desert's interior quality into the twentieth day's assembly with a specific gift: the teaching that the tamed interior life overflows into the taming of the external world. The lion who served Gerasimus after being healed, who worked alongside the monastery's donkey, who mourned at the elder's grave and died there — this is the tradition's way of saying that the holy man who has made peace with his own passions participates in the eschatological peace of creation, that the interior order restored by grace is not confined to the soul but radiates outward into the created order. The desert father who tends his lion is doing, in miniature, what the prophets foresaw for the age to come: the wolf dwelling with the lamb, the lion eating straw like the ox.

St. Artemius was a military official of Alexandria who was martyred under Julian the Apostate — one of the later wave of martyrs, killed not in the era of wholesale persecution but in the specific cruelty of a pagan emperor's targeted campaign against the Church's leading figures. Artemius had been associated with the Arian controversy on the wrong side before his conversion to orthodoxy, which makes his martyrdom a testimony of the restored conscience: a man who had been wrong, who acknowledged it, who redirected his considerable energy to the orthodox cause, and who ultimately died for it. In the month of the Covenant of Mercy, Artemius is the testimony that the mercy is available even for those who have been on the wrong side — that the covenant is open to the corrected conscience as fully as to the one that never needed correction.

The Flight into Egypt, Gerasimus, and Artemius together on the twentieth day of Tiqimt present the interior life in its relationship to guidance, to creation, and to correction. Joseph's interior guidance shows that the divine address does not require spectacular conditions to be real. Gerasimus's lion shows that interior order overflows into exterior harmony. Artemius's corrected conscience shows that the Covenant of Mercy holds the one who has changed direction with the same firmness as the one who never left the path. Tiqimt's second half deepens around these three, and the community moving through the month's final ten days receives them as assurances that the interior life — however it has been conducted, however it may have strayed — is met always by the same mercy that the month is named for.
